results.possibleIdentity: Paul Stewart Kopel (1934–2017) was an electrical engineer and polymath who worked at Bell Telephone Laboratories in Murray Hill, New Jersey, during the 1960s and 1970s. He earned an engineering degree from City College of New York and an advanced engineering degree from NYU. His technical contributions at Bell Labs spanned several fields, including digital system design and mobile radio systems; he co-authored papers on multiaperture reluctance switches (1961) and experimental UHF dual-diversity receivers (1966). Kopel was also noted for his deep interest in languages—being fluent in Italian and studying Russian and Danish—which aligns with his professional involvement in the computer processing of language. Reflecting the mission of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, he was both a technical expert and an artist; he was an accomplished craftsperson and a musician who performed with the Golden Link folk singing society and was associated with modern horological design. After his tenure at Bell Labs, he continued his engineering career in Rochester, New York, before retiring.
